speaker: interviewer
text: September 19th, 1995. Erno Abelesz. The interviewer is Sharon Tyler. It's in London, England, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sharon Tyler. It's Tuesday, the 19th of September. I'm conducting an interview with Erno Abelesz and we are in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Turning over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Erno Abelesz. Erno is E-R-N-O. Abelesz, A-B-E-L-E-S-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell-- tell me your name at birth and spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name was given to me as Kalman, K-A-L-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Were you known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What's your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the 21st of April, 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's for Erno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age now?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my original birthday is 5th of July, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me the city and country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Kapuvar, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell Kapuvar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kapuvar, K-A-P-U-V-A-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me why there is a difference between your name and your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This happened in 1949 when I left Hungary illegally, just across the border to Austria. And at that time, when I was really 19 year old, I thought I will take my deceased younger brother's papers with me, in case if I get caught, I will be regarded as a juvenile and possibly I won't get charged. And once I arrived in Vienna, I had these papers with me, so I've been registered under that name and date of birth. And it stick with me right when I came to England. And I'm known, up to very date, on my younger brother's name and birth date.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can we talk a bit about the town that you were born in, Kapuvar? Tell me a bit-- a little bit about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Kapuvar is an-- basically an insignificant little place in the northwestern part of Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Jewish community constituted about 5% of the total population, which was 10,000. And so the Jewish population was about 500. And like people were engaged in the normal little traders, craftsmen, professionals. But nothing much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your family's business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a grocery wholesaler, which was established in the last century by my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was involved in that-- that as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was-- I mean, we had six children. She helped in the shop as much as she could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the grocery store servicing the Jewish community?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was the general community, other shopkeepers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was quite a lot of-- you were quite integrated, although-- in a way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very much so. Very much so. I mean, most of our customers were from the little villages, little shopkeepers. They bought their goods from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you sell things for-- for Jewish families, too? Was it kosher food as well or a mixture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, whatever we regarded as kosher was a basic food, which is-- which don't have to be kosher. But it was not a specific Jewish shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me about your-- who comprised your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents and six children, one girl and five boys. I was fifth. I was one but the youngest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the age range of the-- of your brothers and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: At 1944-- I mean, when we were deported, my oldest brother was 22. My sister was 21. And then 19, 16, 14, and 11. I was 14.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like for you being the fifth out of six-- six children in the family? What did that mean in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it means that I got-- I got the like cast-over clothes from the older brothers. That was a disadvantage. I very rarely got something new.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any advantage in being the fifth out of the six?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It's not-- no advantage, because I was not the youngest, I should be called the little favorite. And I'm not-- nevertheless, I was down on the line. So I was not-- I couldn't remember any advantage being number five.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your family life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. Very loving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With the extended family, I had a great aunt living with us, my grandfather's maiden sister. And then, at the same time, we had-- we had uncles and aunts, a lot of cousins. Quite a-- quite a large family. One of the largest families in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all lived in Kapuvar?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't say all, but quite a number of them lived in Kapuvar. Usually my-- from the paternal side, Abeleszes. There was-- there was four Abeleszes in Kapuvar besides my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what kind of child would you say you were? How would you describe yourself when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very naughty. That's-- that's how I remember. I was always-- I was very good in school, in the subject of studying. But I was very misbehaved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me an example of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the usual things, scrounging apples from next door and making fun of the teacher when he was not looking. Sometimes I got caught and then I got a good telling off. I loved fun and I loved sports. Generally, I said, a little-- misbehaved little boy can be described. But I was very good in academical studies. I really loved books, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you read a lot, did you, as a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I read a lot. Whatever I could get hold of, I read it. I've still got this habit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was your Jewish life in Kapuvar?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish life, though we were Orthodox, not extreme Hasidische Orthodox, but-- but very Orthodox. But the main community was quite a number of with liberal views. The community itself has been described as Orthodox community.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a Jewish school. But besides the Jewish school, those-- those children who were more religious, they had their special Jewish instructions. As a Jewish school, was given very little what we call Hebrew studies, because it was a-- it was a state school. It was just nominally called Jewish, as the children were all Jewish. So where did you get your Hebrew instruction from? Where did that come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after and before school, what is called a cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the cheder was attached to the synagogue that you belonged to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the synagogue like? What do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The synagogue like was a beautiful synagogue, which was given by Count Esterhazy in the last century to the community. That was the main synagogue where the rabbi and where my parents used to worship. And there was also a-- in later years, opened up a Hasidische shtiebel. And the children were more enticed to the-- more to the Hasidische place, because it was more warm. And so my older brothers, we were more under the influence of the Hasidische, though my parents were strictly kept to the shul, to the main shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything specific that-- any specific incidents that you remember about the Hasidische, the synagogue, or about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was-- there was a great fight between the two parties, those who were Orthodox and those who are more Orthodox. In fact, there was-- sometimes in the '30s, there was two separate shochtim-- ritual slaughterers. One was not satisfied with the others. And in the later years, it has been already quietened down, especially during the war years. There were other things to worry about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and it affected-- I mean, it sounds like you remember it quite clearly. Did it make quite a mark on the-- sort of quite a big thing in the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I was-- I was-- I remember it as a child. There was two parties, like almost like two political parties. One's called the [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. One was a rabbi with a shul and one was a rabbi with a shtiebel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your family also divided about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family were split-- were split in the middle. Some belonged-- my-- some belongs to the original one. Some belongs to the secessionists.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you remember things about how the festivals were celebrated in your-- in the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I do remember every festival. I mean, festivals, the Jewish festivals, are the main highlights of the year. And it was beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit more about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember for Passover, when-- when we baked the matzos in the communal bakery. And in the autumn festivals, when the whole-- everybody came together in the shuls. And after the last of the day of the festival, where they were throwing sweets and nuts. And it was January and-- different functions on Hanukkah and Purim. This was really the highlight what was-- highlights the gray week days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there other things that you remember sort of notably about your childhood? What are the things that sort of stand out for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My childhood-- I mean, I mean, the whole year, school and cheder, quite a-- quite a-- quite a hard regime of studying. In the summer, there was a long pause of approximately two months where the children, we went to our grandparents, who lived not very far from our town. That's why we spent a few weeks of the summer in turn with other cousins, because they couldn't cope with all the grandchildren. That was our holidays. But otherwise, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until the-- until the end of the war, I've never left Kapuvar except for these short holidays in the summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that the Jewish community was-- was integrated-- fairly well integrated in the town. Were most of your friends Jewish or did you have non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not-- not so much socially. Socially, we were not integrated at all. I mean-- I mean, this was quite separately. But-- but as far as respected, or-- the business people in our town, especially my family, were respected by the non-Jewish people as honest-- honest traders. And the professional people, they were the best doctors, the best solicitors. And we had quite a good relationship with the non-Jewish community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitism usually was only from other schoolchildren, from the non-Jewish schoolchildren, sort of Jew-baiting, name calling, which we ignored.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they-- what do you remember yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you have to be-- have to be careful, especially-- especially on Easters, when there was all-- from the Catholic church, there was a procession. And then they said the Jewish people should better stay indoors, because-- because a priest usually talked about it, how-- how their God has been crucified. And-- and they were-- some of the ignorant persons, they were liable to break some Jewish windows. But otherwise, I haven't felt-- myself, I never involved an anti-Semitic incident, though there was. There was. I heard about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't something particularly that directly affected you, except a little bit--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It didn't affect me directly.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the schoolchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: The childhood generally was quite happy and contented childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about when the-- the war began? What did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My first experience as a child, I remember-- I remember the Anschluss, which was 1938, where Austria was occupied by the Germans. And really, I remember the worried faces in the synagogue. And when I asked what-- what went on, then they said that the-- that the Germans had occupied Austria, which is just our next door, considering we were very close to the Austrian border. And by that time, we knew about Hitler, because that was one of the-- how they were taunting us, the non-Jewish children. Hitler is coming. Hitler Is coming. And-- and I had in childhood a fear that there's somebody horrible, Hitler, who eventually is going to come. This was-- this was-- this is a point which has overshadowed my happy childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did-- when were you aware of that starting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I-- as I've grown older, I've learned more and more what is going on. And by-- by certain-- and as per previous years, there was restrictions on Jews. Then we realized it that we are-- we are second-grade citizens and things are not getting better for the Jews as such. And the Jewish people were not accepted in the army. They had to-- they had to cordon in military age into labor battalions under the supervision of the Hungarian army. And the restrictions on businesses, certain licenses have been taken away, restrictions on the-- on the professions. We felt it already that we are not safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was happening 1939, 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Possibly, it happened before. But I was-- as a child, I was not aware of it. But as-- as I've grown up, my awareness has grown, so I realize it, that-- that it's-- it's Hungary is really not our country. We are here second-class citizens and just hope to pray to God that we will get through the difficult years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents discuss it? Was it talked about at home or it was kept very quiet or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I can't remember that my parents has discussed it with us. But in school, with other friends, with older children, you get information. And also, by that time, I was reading the newspapers, so I was quite-- quite well aware what is going on in-- in Hungary and also generally in the world. I was-- always followed-- followed the world news. I was really-- perhaps, even-- even-- even at an early age, I was-- I was-- I was involved in world-- current affairs and what's happening in the world. I remember-- I remember when the war started-- in-- when the Germans occupied France, later the Russians attacked. The Germans attacked Russia. And there was war in northern Africa. I remember I used to make drawings, how the Allied troops stand and where the German troops. I was very much aware and very much conscious about-- about-- about the war and what's happening around us. But Hungary itself was like a little island. We-- we felt that we are safe there, because, especially in our town, even the war has not made much changes in our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- what did change, if anything? You said not many changes, but what-- did things change?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds of regulations about food. Certain foods went on rationing. That was also not [INAUDIBLE] so much an agricultural country. And what became the refugees, refugees started coming from Poland, Slovakia, and they brought bad news what's happening to the Jews in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I remember as a child, they-- they were not talking a lot. But I realized it that they were sort of saved, saved from a-- from a bad fate, from the general community, the Germans are just killing the Jewish people there and-- we had-- we had even a cousin who escaped from Slovakia who lived in Bratislava. It was not so much talk, especially in front of the children what is going on, when my uncles have been taken away from Slovakia. And-- but I-- the fear was already felt. It was in the air. And of course, I understood it that it's-- we are in danger.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- I haven't asked you what language you spoke as a-- in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Strictly Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- there was no Yiddish spoken, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No Yiddish. I haven't spoken at home Yiddish, though when I learned at cheder and later on in yeshiva, the language of the instruction was Yiddish. And gradually, I got to learn the language. But as not a-- not so much as a living language. It's more like an academical language for the study of the Talmud.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- when things were spoken in your family, you could-- it was always Hungarian, so you could always understand everything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always in Hungarian.

speaker: interviewer
text: --everything that was going on. And do-- as far as you remember, did-- did you believe what you were hearing was going on elsewhere? Did it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you have to believe it. I mean, it was-- it was-- you could see from the faces of the people who escaped that it's something horrible going on. And my cousin, who was-- who must have been in his 20s, who escaped, and he knew that his parents had been taken away and he's the only one who managed to get-- to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Taken away? What does it meant? At that time, we were not certain, but-- but we-- we suspected it's something bad. But we haven't got any clear picture at that time about what later on we found out, the organized killings of the Jewish people in Europe. So it was-- people were coming through and you were hearing little bits and pieces of it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Little bits of stories and from schoolmates, all the people who-- all the [INAUDIBLE] who were older than me and they came-- those who were already in Budapest, they came down and they-- they were more-- more knowledgeable. And I was really very aware of it, in fact, more than my schoolmates, somehow, I feel, because I was somehow-- I-- I don't want to brag about that. I was-- I was really older than my age. I always felt that I'm-- I'm a-- I'm grown up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- caused that, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly-- possibly these kind of circumstances which we found ourselves in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So do you think your brothers and sister were also a little bit more--

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly they knew about it. But somehow-- somehow, this kind of subject, it was not discussed. I mean, we have to pretend. In order to carry on living, you had to pretend that living from day to day, everything is all right for the time being.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't need to slate again here, because it's just a battery change.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- this was a half an hour?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. That was 20 minutes. A battery change.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying about feeling more mature for your-- for your years. Did your schooling carry on as normal during the-- during the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. The schooling went on right-- right almost until the Germans moved into Hungary in 1944. There was no changes in the-- in the curriculum. We were still-- still studying the greatness of our country Hungary. But I realized it by that time, especially when our regular teacher was called in into-- into military service, or rather this labor service, which I mentioned before. And there was a substitute teacher who taught us. And he was-- he was really a bit more younger, more friendlier to the children. And he-- he explained to us that we are not really Hungarians and we are restricted and we were not-- then I realized it, that our-- the culture of Hungary and the fate of Hungary is-- is not us. Hungary is not us. We are something different than the Hungarians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you hadn't felt that in your-- as a younger child particularly.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the earlier years, I was-- I don't know how it worked, but in my mind, I thought this is not contradictory, being Jewish and being Hungarian. One is complement each other. I think this is a most wonderful thing to be a Hungarian Jew. I was very proud of the Hungarian history. And also about the Jewish history. And this doesn't contradict each other. I-- in my childhood, I had-- I thought it a great tragedy what befall our people was that Hungary was-- was taken away territories from Hungary. Hungary became a [INAUDIBLE] Hungary. And we were always praying for the resurrection of Great Hungary, which we said it in our prayers. And another thing, that we are in diaspora as Jews. But I hadn't found anything contradictory in these two different terms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it sounds like it came as quite a shock to you when you suddenly--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- it has not came as a shock, because it's not suddenly it came. It's somehow-- some-- as one grows older and the situation has changed, then I-- then I gradually, gradually realized the situation. But it was not, like you put it, a sudden shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm. Tell me something about-- more about your education. You said that you were learning about the-- the history of Hungary. What else did you-- did your school cover?

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned everything, what normal elementary schools study. I mean, geography, arithmetics, and what's going on-- world history. I was quite-- my favorite subject, always, world history and other people. Geography. I was very much interested in that. That was our secular studies. And parallel, we learned specific Jewish studies as I got grown older. By the age of 13, I believe, I-- I started studying in yeshiva full time, which was in our town. Only-- only Jewish subject, I mean Talmud.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did the school only go up to 13 or you left it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the school went up to 14, but somehow I left at 13, because-- because the last class, there was not enough people. I don't know. Some of the children went to a-- went to gymnasium, so there was not enough left, enough children left in the elementary school to have the last-- the  14-year-olds. So I left the school completely. So I really had education up to the-- up to the age of 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was-- what was the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called it just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hungarian Autonomic Orthodox State School.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Autonomic have any meaning in terms of the-- the philosophy at the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think-- I think that defines the Orthodox community which had an autonomic existence. But as far as the subject, we had to-- there was a prescribed by the minister of education what to learn about the general subject-- general secular subject, thrown in a bit of Jewish subjects. And we had to celebrate, the school, all the national Hungarian holidays, like the 15th of March, when we had to wear something-- colored ribbons. That's the Independence Day. And always have the greatest respect to our great leader Horthy. Even the rabbi came to the school to-- to make a sermon about-- about how significant day it is, the Hungarian independence. And I realized that by that time, this is something-- nothing to be proud of, of being-- being Hungarian, and we are not really Hungarian. But-- but you had to pretend. We were-- we were trying at that time to show to the non-Jewish people that we are-- we are as good Hungarians as they are. In fact, my father served in the-- in the Austro-Hungarian army in the First World War, and many other adults. They were good soldiers and good Hungarians and good citizens, as far as being citizens of Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you left your school at 13 to go to-- and went to yeshiva. What did the-- was it just a boys' school or was it a--

speaker: interviewee
text: The school it was-- itself was mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was mixed. So what happened to the girls at 13?

speaker: interviewee
text: The girls, they usually stayed at home. Usually, they learned up to-- up to 14. I think the girls, there was more girls there in the class. So it was really with the boys who were the problem. But the girls, they learned up to the age of 14. And then I think just--
